description: A natural opioid from the poppy and the reference painkiller for severe pain, acting on µ-opioid receptors. The liver converts it to two glucuronides in parallel — M6G, which is itself a potent painkiller, and M3G, which is not — and both are cleared by the kidneys.

halfLife.conditions: EFFECTIVE (functional) half-life = ln2·Vd/CL from Hasselström & Säwe 1993's directly-measured clearance (21.1 mL/min/kg = 1.27 L/h/kg) and volume of distribution (2.9 L/kg): 0.693 × 2.9 / 1.27 = 1.58 h. This matches the FDA morphine-injection label's 'accepted elimination half-life in normal subjects is 1.5 to 2 hours' and carries essentially all of morphine's AUC. The SAME Hasselström study, with a sensitive assay, also resolves a much longer, LOW-AMPLITUDE terminal (~15.1 h); that deep phase is documented but NOT modelled here, because modelling 15 h as a one-compartment half-life would be inconsistent with the measured clearance (it would force CL ~10× too low and inflate AUC ~10×). Reporting the effective phase is the honest one-compartment choice (the same terminal-half-life honesty posture as remifentanil/nicotine, and the dual-half-life posture as warfarin). See notes; the ~15 h tail is also part of why the glucuronides appear so long-lived (metabolite notes).

vd.conditions: volume of distribution 2.9 ± 0.8 L/kg (Hasselström & Säwe 1993, healthy volunteers), within the FDA injection label's apparent Vd range of 1.0–4.7 L/kg. Morphine is only ~36% plasma-protein-bound (FDA label: 'protein binding is low, about 36%'). Sets C(0) = Dose/Vd for an IV bolus (10 mg IV → ~49 ng/mL at 70 kg). Scaled to absolute litres against the 70 kg reference subject (≈203 L).

F.conditions: Oral bioavailability 29.2 ± 7.2% (Hasselström & Säwe 1993 — the SAME healthy-volunteer population as the disposition and both formation fractions). The ~71% that does not reach the circulation as parent is extensive FIRST-PASS glucuronidation in gut and liver; that pre-systemic mass is now represented per metabolite by the additive firstPassFraction (ffp) term rather than simply vanishing. F is the SYSTEMIC parent fraction and is left UNSHAVED — ffp is added on top (shaving F too would double-count the same extracted mass). Drives both the parent oral curve (C ~ F·Dose/Vd) and the systemic metabolite formation (fm·F term).

tmax.conditions: Time to peak of PARENT morphine after immediate-release oral morphine sulfate in healthy fasting adults (median 0.75–1.25 h across Sevredol/Actiskenan/Oramorph references; 0.8 h used, from which ka is derived). Absorption rate is a population-robust parameter, so it is sourced separately from the disposition; F (the load-bearing oral input) stays single-population Hasselström. This parent ka also paces the metabolites' pre-systemic (ffp) Bateman terms — see the metabolite firstPassFraction notes and the routes note below for the timing caveat that follows.

metabolites.name: Morphine-3-glucuronide (M3G)
metabolites.description: The major glucuronide of morphine and the single largest product of its metabolism — yet it has no painkilling activity (and may be mildly excitatory). Like M6G it is cleared by the kidneys.

fractionFormed.conditions: Hasselström & Säwe 1993: the clearance of morphine to form M3G is 57.3% of morphine's total clearance — a MOLAR fraction (fraction of morphine molecules taking that path). The engine's fm multiplies parent MASS eliminated (dA_m/dt = fm·CL·C_p, in mg, no MW factor), so it must be MW-adjusted. M3G (MW 461.46) is HEAVIER than morphine (285.34) — glucuronidation adds a glucuronic-acid group — so each mg of morphine cleared this way yields 461.46/285.34 = 1.617 mg of M3G: mass-fm = 0.573 × 1.617 = 0.927 = 92.7%. This is the DOMINANT pathway (M3G ≫ M6G, ~5.5:1 by mass here). derived: true (molar figure MW-adjusted), the cotinine/caffeine mass-fraction convention. NOTE the two glucuronides' mass-fm sum (92.7 + 16.8 = 109.5%) EXCEEDS 100% — this is expected and correct for metabolites heavier than the parent (the underlying MOLAR fractions sum to 67.7% < 100%); it is not double-counting. This fm drives the SYSTEMIC formation on every route; the ORAL first-pass share is carried separately by firstPassFraction below.

firstPassFraction.conditions: Pre-systemic (first-pass) fraction of an ORAL morphine dose converted to M3G before reaching the circulation — the mass that oral bioavailability (F = 29.2%) loses to gut/hepatic glucuronidation, which the systemic-formation term (fm·CL·C_p) cannot produce. Derived from the SAME single population (Hasselström 1993): of the fraction lost to first pass (1 − F = 0.708 of the dose), the share taking the 3-glucuronidation path is its molar formation-clearance fraction 57.3%, so ffp(molar) = 0.573 × 0.708 = 0.406; MW-adjusted to the engine's mass basis (×461.46/285.34 = ×1.617) gives 0.406 × 1.617 = 0.656 = 65.6%. Purely ADDITIVE to the systemic term — F is NOT also shaved (that would double-count the same extracted mass). CONSTRUCTION IDENTITY (an engine-wiring check, NOT a validation of the ffp value): because ffp is DEFINED as fm·(1−F), the total oral coefficient fm·F + ffp = fm·F + fm·(1−F) = fm algebraically (0.927 × 0.292 + 0.656 = 0.927 = the IV mass-fm); the built curves reproducing this (oral M3G AUC 2.159 ≈ IV 2.158) confirms the oral systemic + pre-systemic paths sum with no coding error, but says nothing about whether ffp itself is right. That rests on the SELECTIVITY ASSUMPTION that first-pass UGT2B7 partitions M3G:M6G like systemic clearance does. It does not exactly: first pass is purely HEPATIC, so the ~10% renal-unchanged escape that dilutes systemic glucuronidation cannot occur pre-systemically — real first-pass glucuronidation is a somewhat larger share, so ffp here is a MILD UNDER-estimate and real oral glucuronide exposure is modestly ABOVE the IV level, not exactly equal. Kept at the systemic ratio (within the [50,80] range) for single-source fidelity; the direction is noted, not hidden. MASS BALANCE holds: ffp_M3G + ffp_M6G (molar 0.406 + 0.074 = 0.480) ≤ 1 − F − f_unabsorbed (0.708; morphine is essentially fully absorbed), the residual 0.229 being first-pass morphine to non-glucuronide pathways (the ~32% 'other' clearance). Oral route ONLY — IV bypasses first pass, so the IV curve is byte-identical to before this pass. TIMING CAVEAT: routed at the parent's ka, so this pre-systemic line peaks near the parent Tmax (~1 h); see notes.

vd.conditions: M3G's OWN volume of distribution, 23.1 L (≈0.33 L/kg), directly measured after INTRAVENOUS M3G (7.5–30 mg/70 kg in 9 healthy volunteers; Penson 2001) — a true metabolite volume, not an apparent Vd/F. Small, as expected for a polar, low-lipophilicity glucuronide (little tissue distribution). Penson fit a two-compartment model; modelled one-compartment here (the metabolite engine's scope). Absolute litres.

halfLife.conditions: M3G's OWN elimination half-life 1.66 ± 0.47 h, measured after direct IV M3G in healthy volunteers with normal renal function (Penson 2001; clearance 169 mL/min). This is MUCH SHORTER than the ~11.2 h APPARENT half-life seen for M3G after MORPHINE dosing (Hasselström) — see the compound notes: after morphine, M3G is formation-rate-limited by morphine's slow deep phase and undergoes enterohepatic recycling, mechanisms a systemic superposition engine cannot represent, so its intrinsic ~1.7 h elimination is used here. M3G is renally cleared and its true elimination lengthens markedly in renal impairment.

metabolites.notes:
Morphine-3-glucuronide is the PRINCIPAL metabolite of morphine (hepatic UGT2B7 glucuronidation at the 3-position) and the largest single product of its metabolism, but it is NOT analgesic — it lacks µ-opioid activity and has been implicated in neuroexcitatory effects (hyperalgesia, myoclonus) at high concentrations. It is drawn (dashed, marked 'metabolite', not 'active metabolite') alongside the active M6G to make the parallel-glucuronidation teaching pair: one dominant inactive product and one minor active one, formed simultaneously off the shared parent clearance (fm_i·CL·C_p).

DISPOSITION — TRUE vs APPARENT (the honesty point). The stored half-life (1.66 h) and volume (23.1 L) are M3G's OWN, measured by giving M3G intravenously (Penson 2001). After MORPHINE, M3G instead appears to decline with a ~11.2 h apparent half-life (Hasselström); that long tail is NOT M3G's elimination but a blend of (a) formation-rate limitation by morphine's low-amplitude ~15 h deep phase and (b) enterohepatic recirculation of the glucuronide — neither representable by this linear systemic engine. Storing the intrinsic ~1.7 h keeps the honesty panel truthful (a reader sees M3G's real elimination, which is what lengthens in renal failure), at the cost of a modelled tail shorter than the observed one. The AMOUNT is faithful regardless: AUC_m = fm·Dose/(k_m·Vd_m) is pinned by fm and M3G's own clearance, independent of the parent's disposition. fm is MW-adjusted from the 57.3% molar formation clearance (×461.46/285.34 = ×1.617 → 92.7% mass).

metabolites.name: Morphine-6-glucuronide (M6G)
metabolites.description: The active glucuronide of morphine — a more potent µ-opioid agonist than morphine itself. Because it is cleared by the kidneys, it builds up in people with poor kidney function and contributes much of the pain relief (and the risk) there.

fractionFormed.conditions: Hasselström & Säwe 1993: the clearance of morphine to form M6G is 10.4% of morphine's total clearance — a MOLAR fraction. MW-adjusted for the engine's mass-basis fm exactly as for M3G: M6G (MW 461.46, morphine glucuronidated at the 6-position) is heavier than morphine (285.34), so mass-fm = 0.104 × (461.46/285.34) = 0.104 × 1.617 = 0.168 = 16.8%. This is the MINOR of the two glucuronides (M3G ≫ M6G, ~5.5:1 by mass), but the pharmacologically important one. derived: true (molar MW-adjusted); the sum with M3G exceeding 100% is expected for heavier metabolites (see the M3G fm note). Drives SYSTEMIC formation; the ORAL first-pass share is carried by firstPassFraction below.

firstPassFraction.conditions: Pre-systemic (first-pass) fraction of an ORAL morphine dose converted to M6G, derived exactly as for M3G from the same single population (Hasselström 1993): of the fraction lost to first pass (1 − F = 0.708), the 6-glucuronidation share is its molar formation-clearance fraction 10.4%, so ffp(molar) = 0.104 × 0.708 = 0.0736; MW-adjusted ×1.617 = 0.119 = 11.9%. Additive to the systemic term (F not shaved). INTERNAL CHECK: fm·F + ffp = 0.168 × 0.292 + 0.119 = 0.168 = the IV mass-fm exactly (the same total M6G forms per dose oral or IV). Oral route ONLY (IV bypasses first pass; the IV curve is unchanged). Same ka-driven timing caveat as M3G (see notes).

vd.conditions: M6G's OWN volume of distribution, 29.38 L (≈0.42 L/kg), directly measured after INTRAVENOUS M6G (30 µg/kg, 6 healthy volunteers; Hanna 1991) — a true metabolite volume, not an apparent Vd/F. Small, as expected for a polar glucuronide with little tissue distribution. (Hanna's reported clearance, 187.81 L/h, is internally inconsistent with its own Vd and half-life — 29.38 L / 2.05 h implies ~10 L/h — and is NOT used; only the self-consistent Vd and half-life are taken.) Absolute litres.

halfLife.conditions: M6G's OWN elimination half-life 2.05 h, measured after direct IV M6G in healthy volunteers with normal renal function (Hanna 1991). Much SHORTER than the ~12.9 h APPARENT half-life seen for M6G after MORPHINE dosing (Hasselström): as with M3G, that long observed tail is formation-rate limitation (by morphine's deep ~15 h phase) plus enterohepatic recycling, not M6G's intrinsic elimination — see compound notes. THE CLINICAL POINT: M6G is renally cleared, so in renal impairment its true elimination lengthens dramatically and it ACCUMULATES to high, long-lasting concentrations — the main reason morphine can cause prolonged sedation/respiratory depression in renal failure. That accumulation is a renal-function effect on M6G's own elimination, which is exactly why the intrinsic (not the apparent) half-life is the honest thing to store.

metabolites.notes:
Morphine-6-glucuronide is the ACTIVE metabolite of morphine (hepatic UGT2B7 glucuronidation at the 6-position): a µ-opioid agonist MORE potent than morphine itself. Although it is the minor glucuronide (~1/5.5 the mass of M3G formed), it contributes substantially to analgesia — and, in renal impairment where it accumulates, to toxicity. Drawn as a dashed 'active metabolite' line beside the inactive M3G: the two together are the parallel-glucuronidation centrepiece (M6G active + M3G inactive, formed simultaneously off the shared parent clearance).

DISPOSITION — TRUE vs APPARENT (see the M3G note for the full argument). Stored half-life 2.05 h and volume 29.38 L are M6G's OWN, from direct IV M6G (Hanna 1991); the ~12.9 h apparent half-life after morphine reflects formation-rate limitation by morphine's low-amplitude deep phase plus enterohepatic recycling (unmodelable here). The intrinsic ~2 h is stored so the honesty panel is truthful and the renal-accumulation teaching (M6G's own elimination is what slows in renal failure) stays coherent. AUC is faithful via AUC_m = fm·Dose/(k_m·Vd_m). fm MW-adjusted from the 10.4% molar formation clearance (×1.617 → 16.8% mass).

hasselstrom_1993.title: Hasselström J, Säwe J. Morphine pharmacokinetics and metabolism in humans. Enterohepatic cycling and relative contribution of metabolites to active opioid concentrations. Clin Pharmacokinet. 1993;24(4):344–354 (PMID 8491060). Healthy volunteers: systemic clearance 21.1 ± 3.4 mL/min/kg (1.27 L/h/kg), volume of distribution 2.9 ± 0.8 L/kg, terminal half-life 15.1 ± 6.5 h (sensitive assay), oral bioavailability 29.2 ± 7.2%; clearance of morphine to form M3G 57.3% and to M6G 10.4% of total clearance; apparent half-lives of morphine, M3G and M6G 15.1, 11.2 and 12.9 h.

fda_label.title: Morphine Sulfate Injection — DailyMed Structured Product Label, section 12.3 Pharmacokinetics (apparent volume of distribution 1.0–4.7 L/kg; total plasma clearance 0.9–1.2 L/kg/h; protein binding low, about 36%; accepted elimination half-life 1.5–2 h; hepatic glucuronidation, chiefly to morphine-3-glucuronide, which is pharmacologically inactive; 2–12% excreted unchanged in urine).

hanna_1991.title: Hanna MH, Peat SJ, Woodham M, Knibb A, Fung C. Disposition of morphine-6-glucuronide and morphine in healthy volunteers. Br J Anaesth. 1991;66(1):103–107 (PMID 1997044). After IV M6G 30 µg/kg (6 healthy volunteers): volume of distribution 29.38 L, elimination half-life 2.05 h.

penson_2001.title: Penson RT, Joel SP, Bakhshi K, Clark SJ, Langford RM, Slevin ML. Limited phase I study of morphine-3-glucuronide. J Pharm Sci. 2001;90(11):1810–1816 (PMID 11745739). Three cohorts of three healthy volunteers received IV M3G 7.5, 15 and 30 mg/70 kg: elimination half-life 1.66 ± 0.47 h, clearance 169 ± 48 mL/min, volume of distribution 23.1 ± 4.8 L (two-compartment fit); virtually no cardiovascular/respiratory effects.

atrux_tallau_2022.title: Atrux-Tallau N, Naimi Z, Jaudinot EO. Pharmacokinetics of Morphine Sulfate Orodispersible Tablets and Bioequivalence with Immediate-Release Oral Morphine Sulfate Formulations in Healthy Adult Subjects Under Fasting Conditions: Single-Dose Comparative Bioavailability Studies. Clin Drug Investig. 2022;42(12):1101–1112 (PMID 36331670). Single-dose fasting healthy adults, 5/10/30 mg; immediate-release references Sevredol/Actiskenan/Oramorph — parent morphine median Tmax 0.75–1.25 h (30 mg Sevredol Cmax 28.5 ng/mL). Used only for the oral parent Tmax (→ ka).

osborne_1990.title: Osborne R, Joel S, Trew D, Slevin M. Morphine and metabolite behaviour after different routes of morphine administration: demonstration of the importance of the active metabolite morphine-6-glucuronide. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 1990;47(1):12–19 (PMID 2295214). After ORAL morphine the plasma AUC ratios M3G:morphine ≈ 30 and M6G:morphine ≈ 3.6 (molar) — far higher than after IV, demonstrating extensive first-pass glucuronidation. Used only as an oral-route magnitude cross-check for the glucuronide lines (not a stored-parameter source).

metabolism: The liver enzyme UGT2B7 glucuronidates morphine at two positions in parallel: the 3-position gives morphine-3-glucuronide (M3G, the major product, with no painkilling activity), and the 6-position gives morphine-6-glucuronide (M6G, minor but a more potent painkiller than morphine itself). Both are cleared by the kidneys and accumulate in kidney failure — which is why M6G in particular can cause prolonged effects in people with poor renal function. Both the intravenous and the oral route are shown, and the contrast between them is the point. Given intravenously, morphine reaches the bloodstream whole and is glucuronidated there. Taken by mouth, only about 29% of a dose reaches the circulation intact — most is glucuronidated on its FIRST PASS through the gut wall and liver before ever entering the bloodstream. That first-pass conversion is drawn as a fast early rise of both glucuronides (they appear even as the parent line stays low), so oral dosing produces far more M3G/M6G relative to morphine than intravenous dosing does — the same total amount of each glucuronide forms either way, but by mouth much of it forms up front. Two caveats on the shapes: (1) the first-pass rise is timed to the parent's absorption, so it peaks a little earlier than the real glucuronide peak (their appearance is also paced by the slow conversion step); and (2) each glucuronide is drawn declining with its own intrinsic ~2 h half-life, but after real morphine dosing they linger for ~11–13 h because their formation is paced by a slow deep phase of morphine and because they are recycled through the gut (enterohepatic cycling) — mechanisms this linear model does not include.
notes:
The parallel-glucuronidation teaching centrepiece: one parent forming TWO metabolites simultaneously — M3G (major, inactive) and M6G (minor, active/more potent than morphine) — exercising the N-metabolite engine with a two-line active/inactive contrast (cf. caffeine's three parallel demethylation products).

SINGLE-POPULATION SOURCING. Morphine's disposition and BOTH glucuronide formation fractions come from ONE healthy-volunteer study (Hasselström & Säwe 1993): CL 21.1 mL/min/kg, Vd 2.9 L/kg, and clearances to M3G (57.3%) and M6G (10.4%) of total. Each glucuronide's OWN disposition comes from a study that administered that glucuronide intravenously (Hanna 1991 for M6G; Penson 2001 for M3G) — true metabolite volumes and half-lives, not apparent Vd/F.

MODEL — ONE-COMPARTMENT EFFECTIVE PARENT. Morphine is genuinely multi-exponential, but its measured CL and Vss correspond to an EFFECTIVE half-life of ln2·Vd/CL = 1.58 h (≈ the FDA label's 'effective ~2 h'), which carries essentially all of its AUC. The sensitive-assay terminal (~15.1 h) is a low-amplitude deep phase: it CANNOT be used as a one-compartment half-life, because pairing 15 h with Vss 2.9 L/kg implies CL ~0.13 L/h/kg — ~10× below the measured 1.27 — so it would inflate AUC tenfold. Morphine's macro-parameters are mutually consistent only for the effective phase, so one-compartment at 1.58 h is the honest representation; the 15 h tail is documented, not modelled (remifentanil/nicotine terminal-honesty and warfarin dual-half-life posture). Clearance is not stored (t½ is derived from CL/Vd — storing CL too would be circular).

METABOLITE DISPOSITION — THE KEY CURATION DECISION (advisor-reviewed). The glucuronides' APPARENT half-lives after morphine (M3G 11.2 h, M6G 12.9 h; Hasselström) are much LONGER than their INTRINSIC elimination half-lives measured after direct IV glucuronide (M3G 1.66 h, Penson; M6G 2.05 h, Hanna). The long apparent tails are formation-rate limitation (the glucuronides are paced by morphine's slow low-amplitude ~15 h deep phase) PLUS enterohepatic recirculation — neither representable by a linear systemic superposition engine. Three options were weighed: (A) store the true intrinsic disposition; (B) store the apparent ~12 h as the metabolite's 'elimination'; (C) build a multi-compartment parent carrying the 15 h phase to drive the tails. B was rejected because it would corrupt the honesty panel (presenting a formation/enterohepatic artefact as M6G's elimination) and break the renal-accumulation teaching (M6G matters because its OWN elimination slows in renal failure). C was rejected because the 15 h phase is ~1% amplitude (so it would not reproduce the observed tail anyway) and enterohepatic cycling is still unmodelable — a fabricated, ill-conditioned parent for no gain. OPTION A shipped: store each glucuronide's true, measured, single-population elimination and Vd. The metabolite AMOUNTS are faithful (AUC_m = fm·Dose/(k_m·Vd_m), independent of parent disposition); only the shape's long tail — the unmodelable part — is shortened, and that discrepancy is turned into the teaching caveat in the metabolism prose and the per-metabolite notes.

fm — MASS CONVERSION. Hasselström's formation clearances are MOLAR fractions; the engine's fm multiplies parent MASS, and both glucuronides (MW 461.46) are HEAVIER than morphine (285.34), so mass-fm = molar × 461.46/285.34 = molar × 1.617: M3G 0.573 → 92.7%, M6G 0.104 → 16.8%. Their sum exceeds 100% (109.5%) — correct for heavier metabolites (molar sum 67.7% < 100%), the inverse of caffeine's lighter dimethylxanthine metabolites and the same direction as cotinine (×1.086).

ROUTES — ORAL + IV BOLUS (oral added this pass via the first-pass engine). Oral was previously DEFERRED here on the oseltamivir/nicotine criterion: morphine's ~29% oral bioavailability (Hasselström) reflects extensive FIRST-PASS glucuronidation in gut and liver, so much of the M3G/M6G forms PRE-systemically — which the old systemic-formation-only engine could not represent (it drew the oral glucuronide lines far too low). The firstPassFraction (ffp) engine capability now represents exactly that: a fraction ffp of the oral dose enters the metabolite compartment directly, as an oral-absorption input at the parent's ka, additive to the systemic fm·CL·C_p term. So oral morphine now draws faithfully and is the centrepiece example of the general case — BOTH systemic glucuronidation (fm > 0) AND first-pass glucuronidation (ffp > 0) — not the degenerate purely-pre-systemic shape. DEFAULT LANDING ROUTE is now ORAL (defaultRoute picks the first derivable+available route in ENGINE_ROUTES order, oral first) — an intentional change from the prior iv-bolus-only default: oral is morphine's dominant real-world route and the first-pass glucuronide contrast is this compound's headline teaching point, and it matches every other oral+IV compound (allopurinol/acetaminophen also open on oral). The IV-bolus curve is unchanged and one click away.

WHY MORPHINE AND NOT OSELTAMIVIR (the first-pass timing screen). The ffp term routes the pre-systemic mass at the parent's absorption rate ka — i.e. it assumes hepatic conversion is FAST relative to absorption, so the metabolite Tmax ≈ parent Tmax. Morphine passes this screen: glucuronidation is one of the fastest phase-II reactions, and after morphine the glucuronides peak close behind the parent (subcutaneous morphine: M3G Tmax ~0.25 h, M6G ~0.6 h; oral IR parent Tmax ~0.75–1.25 h). Oseltamivir was EVALUATED first as the flagship ffp compound and REJECTED on this screen: its carboxylate is formation-rate-limited (Tmax ~6 h ≫ parent ~0.5 h), an ~8–12× timing violation on the dominant line that the single-ka instant-conversion model would invert (early spike instead of slow rise) — a refuse-don't-mislead case, deferred pending a metabolite-input-rate-decoupled ffp extension. The reusable rule (docs/DATA_GUIDE.md): before curating any ffp pair, check metabolite Tmax ≈ parent Tmax — the ffp analogue of the F·D/V ceiling test.

ffp — DERIVATION + MASS BALANCE (advisor-reviewed). Each glucuronide's ffp comes from the SAME single population as its fm (Hasselström 1993): the pre-systemic fraction is the first-pass loss (1 − F = 0.708 of the dose) apportioned by that glucuronide's molar formation-clearance share (assuming first-pass UGT2B7 selectivity matches systemic), then MW-adjusted to mass exactly like fm (×1.617): M3G 0.573 × 0.708 × 1.617 = 65.6%, M6G 0.104 × 0.708 × 1.617 = 11.9%. F is left UNSHAVED (ffp is additive; shaving F too would double-count). Because ffp is DEFINED as fm·(1−F), the total oral coefficient fm·F + ffp collapses to fm algebraically for each glucuronide (M3G 0.927, M6G 0.168) — a CONSTRUCTION IDENTITY, not an independent validation; the built curves matching it (oral M3G AUC 2.159 ≈ IV 2.158) is an engine-wiring check that the systemic + pre-systemic paths sum correctly. The ffp value's real support is the SELECTIVITY ASSUMPTION (first-pass UGT2B7 splits M3G:M6G like systemic clearance); since first pass is purely HEPATIC and cannot include the ~10% renal-unchanged escape, true first-pass glucuronidation is a slightly larger share — so ffp here is a MILD UNDER-estimate and real oral glucuronide exposure sits modestly ABOVE the IV level rather than exactly equal. Mass balance closes: molar ffp_M3G + ffp_M6G = 0.480 ≤ 1 − F − f_unabsorbed = 0.708 (morphine is essentially fully absorbed), the 0.229 residual being first-pass morphine to the ~32% non-glucuronide pathways. ffp is an illustrative population constant, not a patient covariate — the bright line holds.

A TIMING CAVEAT REMAINS. The pre-systemic glucuronide line peaks near the parent Tmax (~1 h), somewhat earlier than the real oral glucuronide peak, because the ffp term omits the finite conversion step and (as on IV) draws the intrinsic ~2 h decline rather than the ~11–13 h formation/enterohepatic tail. Magnitude and total exposure are faithful; the shape's timing is approximate — the allopurinol/ketamine posture, but far milder than the oseltamivir case that was rejected.

MAGNITUDE CHECK (built engine curves). IV: 10 mg IV morphine → C(0) = 10 mg / (2.9 L/kg × 70 kg ≈ 203 L) ≈ 49 ng/mL, consistent with reported post-bolus morphine. The glucuronides rise behind the parent and, being (weakly) elimination-rate-limited relative to the fast effective parent (metabolite t½ 1.7–2 h > parent 1.6 h), peak a few hours later and linger modestly past it; M3G is the larger line (~5.5× M6G by AUC), reflecting the dominant 3-glucuronidation pathway. ORAL: ~22.5 mg morphine base (≈30 mg morphine sulfate) → modelled parent Cmax ~22.8 ng/mL @0.8 h, bracketing the reported 28.5 ng/mL (Atrux-Tallau 2022, 30 mg Sevredol); the parent oral AUC (~74 ng·h/mL) runs below the reported ~117 because the model draws the EFFECTIVE 1.58 h phase, not the sensitive-assay tail (documented). GLUCURONIDE MAGNITUDE (the dominant lines — checked against reported ratios, not asserted): modelled oral M3G:morphine AUC ratio ≈ 29 and M6G:morphine ≈ 5 (mass basis). Osborne 1990 reports oral M3G:morphine ≈ 30 (molar) / ≈ 50 (mass) and M6G:morphine ≈ 3.6 (molar) / ≈ 9 (mass). So the model sits AT or BELOW the reported glucuronide:parent ratio — the glucuronide TOTAL EXPOSURE is conservative (under-drawn), consistent with the mild-under-estimate direction above plus the un-modelled enterohepatic recycling that inflates the real glucuronide AUC. The modelled M3G Cmax (~500 ng/mL) looks tall-and-narrow only because the intrinsic ~1.7 h t½ compresses that conservative AUC into a sharp early peak rather than the real broad, lingering (~11–13 h) profile — the SAME intrinsic-vs-apparent / no-enterohepatic caveat already carried on the IV curve, not a new over-prediction. Net: oral draws the glucuronides towering over the parent (M3G ~22×, M6G ~3× the parent line) — the first-pass teaching contrast — with faithful-to-conservative exposure and an approximate (early, sharp) shape. Educational illustration only — not for dosing.
